items.fullDescription: About the Book Indian Cultural Heritage: Perspectives is an edited volume that invites interdisciplinary scholarship on the diverse and dynamic dimensions of India's cultural heritage. The volume welcomes original contributions exploring tangible and intangible heritage, indigenous knowledge systems, languages, literature, folklore, performing arts, architecture, material culture, cultural memory, museums, heritage conservation, digital heritage, and contemporary issues of identity, sustainability, and cultural preservation. By bringing together diverse academic perspectives, the volume seeks to advance critical understanding of India's rich cultural traditions and their continuing significance in a rapidly changing world.

items.fullDescription: This virtual seminar takes its inspiration from Saidiya Hartman’s citation of the Greek etymology of the word chorus : “to dance within an enclosure.” For Hartman, the chorus also furnishes a paradigm for thinking—to play on Jean-Luc Nancy’s notion—Black (non)being singular plural. In Wayward Lives , Hartman suggests that any of the minor figures can stand in for the others, not as a claim of homogeneity, but rather as a contention that their singularity can be read as paradigmatic of waywardness—in its queer heterogeneity—in general. The chorus of black femme and queer youth is thus at once a singular plurality and a plural singularity as they make beautifully riotous lives within the enclosure of social death.

items.fullDescription: Historically, researching and teaching the different art forms took place in a well-sealed vacuum: for example, using literary theory to decipher one of the great texts, visual and compositional analysis to examine a sublime painting, or montage splicing to perceive the nuances of film. However, it is becoming more difficult to recognize the borders that separate cinema, literature, and the visual arts. Factors including the rise of globalized societies and the emergence of new technology have forced us to reconsider medium specificity and our relationship with creative expressions and works of art.

items.fullDescription: This panel session seeks to explore the cultural fascination surrounding "The Backrooms," an internet 'creepypasta' about an abandoned office building with endless corridors that became a popular YouTube series and most recently, a blockbuster horror film. What are the Backrooms? Do they represent an artificial or alien intelligence attempting to simulate reality? Are they a glitch in the imperfect fabric of an unstable universe? Are they a metaphor for the subconscious, techno-paranoia, wasteland necrocapitalism, corporate control, or the internet itself? Or is this all just a retelling of the Greek myth of the Labyrinth for a Gen Z audience?

items.fullDescription: This roundtable discusses the convergence of literary expression and digital activism as means of empowering a community. The shift of passive representation to active agency in the 21st century can be common in the digital space where the local knowledge is stored and disseminated. We discuss how marginalized groups, namely Dalit writers and activists, have used digital humanities to evade authoritarian gatekeepers and develop transnational solidarities. This session welcomes people to talk about how online spaces can be seen as a form of regenerative space where the testimonios of Dalit can be heard, transforming the world into a supportive space of collective action and literary insurgency.

items.fullDescription: On January 19, 2022, Sheila Heti published the first of ten weekly installments of “A Diary in Alphabetical Order” in The New York Times. Each installment included an author’s note explaining that Heti had “loaded all 500,000 words of [her] journals into Excel to order the sentences alphabetically,” hoping to “see the self in a new way.” Over time, the project “grew into something more novelistic,” and the note concludes that “the self’s report on itself is surely a great fiction.” Heti is hardly the first author to challenge or destabilize autobiographical conventions.

items.fullDescription: Drawing inspiration from Adrienne Rich's seminal book Of Woman Born (1976), in 2006, Andrea O'Reilly coined the term "Motherhood Studies" and established a new interdisciplinary academic field aimed at studying "motherhood" as an institution and "mothering" as an experience. O'Reilly emphasized the importance of adopting a feminist framework designed expressly for mothers: a "matricentric feminism" necessary for an intersectional exploration of motherhood. Within "Motherhood Studies," being or not being a mother matters, and the experience of motherhood is treated as a category alongside gender, class, and race.

Building upon Hortense Spillers’ important essay Mama’s Baby, Papa’s Maybe (1987), we seek papers from humanities scholars in the French and Francophone worlds that reframe the body not as a passive victim but as a living text, a site of memory, and an alternative space that rescripts official records. By retracing histories through the reading of the body, we can activate affect, unleash unexpected memories, and create generative spaces for art that centres on cultural knowledge to empower communities.

items.fullDescription: In the definition given by Podnieks, matrifocal literatures are written and narrated by mothers in the first-person or limited third-person voice, rendering maternal identity and experience from subjective perspectives. Such narratives (re)value the significance and meaning of maternal figures in cultural, social, and national arenas, and often contest or negotiate traditional ideologies of the “good” mother as self-sacrificing, nurturing, and sexless with her antithesis, the “bad” mother. In the last few years, we have seen the rise of matrifocal writings published to break taboos about previously silenced reproductive experiences, create a sense of community, support, and care, and, overall, empower people.

items.fullDescription: In bell hooks’ Belonging: A Culture of Place (2009), she posits that “many folks feel no sense of place” (1). She continues by explaining her personal experience with this spatiality, noting that, “Like many of my contemporaries I have yearned to find my place in this world, to have a sense of homecoming, a sense of being wedded to a place” (2). To that end, a commonality shared between all humans is a searching for a place in which we belong, and this is particularly evident in literature that is produced during times of major societal events.

items.fullDescription: In her resolute commitment to telling stories about Black life without the white gaze, Toni Morrison articulated a model of community empowerment grounded in local knowledge, intra-communal collaboration, and the self-reflexive narration of Black worlds. While Morrison remains one of the most visible public advocates of this aesthetic and ethical orientation, practices of Black worldmaking across African and diasporic literatures have long exceeded mere response to anti-Blackness. Even under conditions structured by racial violence, coloniality, displacement, and social rupture, Black communities have continually generated modes of life, relation, memory, and imagination that assert the possibility of worlds otherwise.

They are: A Thousand Acres by Jane Smiley (a contemporary King Lear ); March by Geraldine Brooks ( Little Women from the perspective of the absent father); Demon Copperhead by Barbara Kingsolver (David Copperfield set in Appalachia); and James by Percival Everett (Adventures of Huckleberry Finn from the perspective of Jim). This panel looks to open a discussion about how these and other contemporary novels both explore and challenge the themes of the source material. What do they say about society, race, gender, feminism, and marginalization?

items.fullDescription: In both her life and her literary work, American writer Edith Wharton was a keen observer and recorder of the interrelationship(s) between power and community at the end of the 19th century and throughout the early decades of the 20th. Born into the world of wealth and privilege of “Old New York” society, Wharton grew weary of its longstanding communal norms and constraints. At the same time, however, she was also openly critical of the ostentation and conspicuous consumption on display during the annual Newport, RI “summer season.”

items.fullDescription: CFP for NeMLA 2027 Conference Virtual Only Panel Oral Community Knowledge as Antidote for Empire in Anglophone Literature If Empire thrives and even conquers through written text: codification of laws and bureaucracy, cartography of territory, travelogues of the exotic Other, it equally seeks to suppress oral indigenous knowledge of the vanquished. Yet orality can also be the site of resistance and community identity for just such a reason. This panel seeks proposals of works that investigate how orality of form and/or content in Anglophone literature disrupts Empire and empowers community. The irony of pursuing orality in literature, which is historically written, is not lost on us, but also provides an opportunity for creative, resilient exploration. Some possible ways of approaching this topic may include: Accent and dialect Pidgins, creoles, and contact languages Translations and translators Language and identity Oral folk wisdom and stories Glossaries and indices Textual hybridity or multimodality Literacy This is a Call for Papers for the NeMLA 2027 Conference. items.relevance: Automatically surfaced as a likely RCID fit across Digital Humanities / Design.
items.fullDescription: Panel Proposal for the NeMLA Convention, Newport, RI, March 6-9, 2027 Planetary crises-climate change, toxic pollution, biodiversity loss, and energy transition-are profoundly uneven in their distribution. Their material consequences are shaped by infrastructures of class, race, colonial histories, state power, and global capitalism. Increasingly, literary, artistic, and cultural texts across the world foreground these asymmetries, framing ecological crisis not only as environmental catastrophe but as a question of justice: Who breathes polluted air? Who profits from green transformation? Who consumes melting ice? Who bears the social and environmental costs of planetary change? This panel invites contributions that explore how texts and cultural practices from diverse linguistic, geographic, and medial contexts engage with the unequal dimensions of ecological crisis. Moving beyond a single literary tradition, the panel seeks to foster a comparative and transnational conversation on environmental justice and planetary inequality. We welcome papers addressing, among others: • Environmental and climate justice across global contexts • Toxicity, pollution, and slow violence • Extractivism and postcolonial ecologies • Infrastructures of energy, water, and air • "Green" transition and its social costs • Migration, borders, and climate displacement • Rural transformation and environmental precarity • Tourism, consumption, and the commodification of crisis • Riverine, oceanic, and littoral ecologies • Extinction, memory, and planetary archives • Nonhuman agency and more-than-human ethics • Intersections of ecology with race, gender, and class • Indigenous, decolonial, and alternative ecological epistemologies We encourage both close readings and theoretically informed approaches drawing on environmental humanities, political ecology, energy humanities, postcolonial studies, infrastructure studies, material ecocriticism, or related fields. Contributions may engage literature, film, visual art, performance, or other cultural forms. The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. Please clearly indicate in your submission whether you wish to participate in person or remotely. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal. Please note that the submission window for individual abstracts officially opens June 15 and closes September 30.

items.relevance: Automatically surfaced as a likely RCID fit across Digital Humanities / Design, Theory, Art / Media Practice.
items.fullDescription: Affect theory has grown largely within Euro-American frameworks, from Berlant's neoliberal America to Stewart's account of the American ordinary. Sara Ahmed's work on the cultural politics of emotion has been pivotal in reorienting the field toward race, migration, and colonial power, showing how feelings attach differently to racialized and gendered bodies. This panel builds on that reorientation. It asks what happens when affect theory travels more fully into postcolonial and diasporic contexts, where the body in question has been shaped by colonial histories, forced migration, and asymmetrical encounters with Western institutions. How do structures of feeling shift under these conditions, and where do existing frameworks reach their limit? The panel takes postcolonial and diasporic texts as active interlocutors, capable of pressuring, revising, and extending theory rather than simply illustrating it. We invite papers that attend to the specific conditions under which feeling circulates, accumulates, and fails to discharge in migrant and postcolonial experience. How does testimony feel from the position of the postcolonial subject? What does care cost in communities shaped by colonial injury and displacement? Objects, spaces, and bodies carry affective residue across cultures and histories in ways that ask for new forms of attention and recognition. Papers might engage fiction, film, digital media, and visual culture from postcolonial and diasporic traditions, attending to the affective dimensions of migrant and colonial experience. We welcome contributions grounded in affect theory, postcolonial studies, comparative literature, and media studies. Graduate researchers and faculty are encouraged to apply.

items.fullDescription: In Aldous Huxley's Brave New World, humans are conditioned to seek out human companionship and to despise being by themselves. Helmholtz Watson, a lecturer of Emotional Engineering, relates a story to Bernard Marx about being disciplined for sharing some of his poems with the students he was paid to indoctrinate. When asked what the poems had been about, Helmholtz tells a surprised Bernard, "They were about being alone." Helmholtz, like many individuals in our world outside the novel, has a natural inclination to seek solitude. Some individuals choose to separate from community for short or long periods of time, retreating into the solitude of reflection and quiet. The flip side of solitude is loneliness, occurring when individuals experience unwanted distance from community and feel the lack of human connection. And then, there is an in-between state of those who simultaneously, and seemingly paradoxically, need copious alone time while also desiring community. This creative session will showcase readings of original works of poetry and prose by writers who compose about being alone, whether from a standpoint of loneliness, solitude, or somewhere in between. Poetry, fiction, and creative nonfiction are welcome in this session. Readers may also present a combination of genres. Paper proposals are accepted through September 30, 2026. Abstracts are 200-300 words in length, and titles should not exceed 100 characters. items.fullDescription: This panel presents a review of the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny, Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio. The panel centers on aspects of African diasporic experiences, i.e., Afro-Latinidad. The panel considers Blackness as gleaned and embedded from Bad Bunny's musical repertoire and uses Aurora Santiago Ortiz and Jorell Melendez-Badillo's framework of "historical memory" and "limits of nostalgia" as presented in their piece "(Nada es) 'Como antes.'" The panel seeks to utilize this framework and focus on Black presence in all its intersecting and non-intersecting forms involving race, class, gender, and sex. Specifically, papers are sought that examine strategies and uses of, for example, an "idyllic past" and/or "Great Mixed Family" motif and show ruptures in representations that subsume or otherize Blackness. Submissions can interpret outside of Bad Bunny and include other artists venturing into its use in other realms and/or aspects of appreciation or appropriation discourse, read broadly and sublimated in text. The panel discusses the uses of various musical, visual, aural, and literary works to deconstruct language use, song beats, films, concerts, and media, similar to Bad Bunny's use of House, Hip Hop, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, Bachata, Afro Punk, etc. A key element is locating, gleaning, or demystifying aspects of Blackness, construed broadly, in Bad Bunny's context or in other works that offer perspectives on Black embeddedness and involve authenticity, appreciation, and the intersection of race, class, gender, or sex. Papers are sought that review the application, infusion, or parallelism of the Black past infusing Bomba, Plena, Son, Salsa, Trap, Drill, Reggaeton, and other genres, as merged running threads. This panel invites papers that explore the musical, artistic, literary, social, political, and cultural expressions of Bad Bunny (Benito Antonio Martinez Ocasio). We focus on African diasporic, or AfroLatinidad, experiences. The panel examines how Blackness construed in various forms appears in Bad Bunny's music and/or is subsumed within it. The panel delves into the intersections of race, class, gender, and sex, and the recognition of the African presence, identity, or representation.

items.relevance: Automatically surfaced as a likely RCID fit across Film / Media, Art / Media Practice.
items.fullDescription: In Spring of 2026, the first stage of NASA's Artemis mission captured photographs of the Earth from space, a collective earthling selfie that momentarily transcended the squalor and congestion of the daily news cycle. While satellite imagery, Google maps, and weather forecasting technology have made representations of the global scale relatively commonplace, the astronaut photos have a human presence that allows them to capture something of the sublimity of what it means to live on a planet. The nature of the planetary perspective is epistemologically elusive, being difficult and maybe impossible to reconcile with the phenomenology of lived experience. It is also inherently polysemous, being open to numerous, ramifying, and conflicting interpretations, indicating both mankind's puniness and its awesome technological power, providing a meta-explanation for the reality of everything while being simultaneously abstract and largely irrelevant to everyday life, suggesting a deep connection between human beings and Earth while also overriding Earth toward the project of discovering new and improved planetary homes. Gayatri Chakrovarty Spivak positions inherent alterity at the center of what she calls "planet-thought": "The planet is in the species of alterity, belonging to another system; and yet we inhabit it, on loan. … If we imagine ourselves as planetary subjects … alterity remains underived from us; it is not our dialectical negation, it contains us as much as it pushes us away." Cinema is the art of representing the world through an uncanny simulacrum of itself, which may make it a particularly apt medium for capturing the ambivalent and paradoxical alterity that defines the ontology of the planetary imagination. Indeed, one of the first works of narrative cinema, Georges Méliès's A Trip to the Moon (1902), is structured around visionary depictions of Earth from space that uncannily anticipate the Apollo and Artemis photos while also inspiring a rich filmic legacy of representations of planetarity. The narrative and imagery of such films has helped to shape the popular perception of what it means to live on a planet, and at their best, these films engage the kinds of contradictions, paradoxes, and ambiguities that Spivak and other cultural studies theorists have explored. This panel will focus on what movies about planets can teach us about what planets are and how to live on them. We accept proposals about movies that foreground telluric-scale events on earth (Armageddon [1998], Don't Look Up [2021], etc.), movies about space exploration (Apollo 13 [1995], Gravity [2013], The Martian [2015], etc.), or movies set on alien planets (Star Wars, Dune, etc.). Any other approaches to examining the intersection between cinema and planetarity are also welcome. Please submit a 300-word abstract by September 30, 2026. Contact Dr. Randy Laist with questions: rlaist@bridgeport.edu.

items.fullDescription: In the four hundred years since its invention in Renaissance Florence, opera has become synonymous with the grandiose, the excessive, and the melodramatic, yet it has only gained a foothold in the academy as an object of serious academic study within the past fifty years. Since then, however, an abundance of scholarship has yielded everything from formal musicological readings of operatic works to theoretical inquiries inspired by psychoanalysis into voice and performance. And topics like the relationship between opera and sovereignty in seventeenth century Italy and the appropriation of Wagner by the Third Reich underscore how opera has never been far from the political sphere in the Western world. But as Slavoj Zizek confirms, "the more opera is dead, the more it flourishes." Opera's story may be one of perpetual decadence, but contemporary creators deserving of academic attention push against the narrative and economic forces that have challenged the validity of opera as an artform in the 21st century. This panel seeks to complement contemporary scholarship on opera across the humanities by soliciting papers that speak to opera's unique, overdetermined, and intrinsically interdisciplinary blend of words, music, performance, and history, as well as papers that examine operatic genealogies extant in non-operatic texts. Potential panelists are also invited to think across languages, borders, and performance traditions and consider the important social and political questions of canonicity, representation, and ownership as they pertain to opera and the operatic.

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers -- NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention. Link: https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22237 March 6-9, 2027. Newport, Rhode Island. Convention Site: Salve Regina University. Session Abtract The guiding thread of this panel develops around a curious remark by Jacques Rancière that would seem to amend a classically Aristotelian thesis: "man is a political animal because he is a literary animal," the fundamental condition of which is the "excess of words" constitutive of the human animal's politicalness. Literature (or poetry) has long been placed in close proximity to politics, as we find in Plato's Republic, where philosophy and literature-and, more generally, philosophy and politics-turn out to be perennial adversaries. That matters of literature bear upon the question of politics has featured in continental French thought for quite some time, considered in terms of the milieu of différance and event in Jacques Derrida; le partage du sensible and dissensus in Jacques Rancière; and the unpresentable and testimony to le différend in Jean-François Lyotard, among others. More recently, Geoffrey Bennington has taken up the question of the unreadable as that condition that keeps reading open to the event of alterity. What is at stake, then, in the complex relationship between literature and politics? How does literature bear witness to, or otherwise reconfigure, who counts as a speaking being, what is recognized as speech, and/or what falls out of discursive legibility as mere noise, excess, or silence? And how might mis-reading, mis-translation, and mis-understanding contribute to the politics of literature? This panel invites reflection on the figures, scenes, and events of textuality that respond to these and related questions. All critical approaches are welcome for this in-person panel. Eric J. Adamo McMaster University Hamilton, Ontario, Canada.

items.fullDescription: This panel invites papers that examine the agency of marginalized characters in literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic productions from the French?speaking world across any historical period. Artistic works offer numerous examples of figures who maintain an ambivalent relationship to their communities. Because of their social or ethnic origins, their divergent perspectives, or their transgression of established norms, certain characters find themselves pushed to the margins of social, familial, or professional groups. While such exclusion may appear to weaken those who experience it, this panel asks whether marginalization might also function as a source of strength. Can marginality become a generative position? Do marginalized individuals succeed in forming new communities that enable them to confront stigma and ultimately overcome it? This discussion seeks to shift our understanding of the place occupied by marginalized individuals and groups in fictional narratives. By considering the networks of solidarity that emerge among marginalized subjects, the panel explores what lies beneath their presumed inferiority, inviting critical attention to what is hidden, erased, or rendered invisible within dominant narratives. More importantly, we will examine how marginalized communities may become empowering spaces of resistance, mutual support, and hope. Drawing on approaches from cultural studies, postcolonial theory, sociolinguistics, and intersectionality, this session seeks to understand how literary, visual, audiovisual, and artistic narratives stage the tensions between marginality and belonging, strength and vulnerability. Proposals engaging with environmental humanities, posthumanism, Indigenous studies, as well as diaspora and migration studies are strongly encouraged. We also welcome submissions drawing on gender and queer studies, memory and trauma studies, digital humanities, or other interdisciplinary frameworks within the humanities. We welcome proposals of 250-300 words, in French or English, submitted by September 30.

items.fullDescription: If you've taught older literature recently, you've probably heard students ask the question "Why am I reading this?" They've got a point. Why should we read Shakespeare's plays, or Paradise Lost , or (god-forbid) "The Rape of the Lock"? We might be able to coax students into reading a Jane Austen novel or even Huckleberry Finn because they are still part of our cultural imagination, but it's less easy to coax students into reading Beowulf or Tom Jones -texts that are less familiar in form and content. As we jettison the canon and the timeless truths of literature, what do we expect reading older texts will give our students that they wouldn't otherwise understand? How do we explain our reasoning to students about why they should read texts from earlier literary periods? How do we present these earlier periods (or periodization) and their culture and ideas as relevant to our undergraduate students in our English, writing, or humanities programs? Or is relevance the problem? How might we redefine older literature in our classes, or what we (can) do with these texts? I'm particularly interested in position papers that take into consideration practical pedagogical and theoretical concerns: arguments about curricula, course development or assignments that do different things with this literature. Papers are welcome on any kind of literature/texts or periods prior to 1800.

items.fullDescription: Collective Selves: Rethinking Autobiography Beyond the Individual. Philippe Lejeune's influential formulation of the autobiographical pact has long shaped autobiography studies. The pact presumes an aligned author, narrator, and protagonist, and simultaneously privileges the singular, first-person life narrative as the normative autobiographical form. However, several personal narratives resist these assumptions. Edited autobiographies, oral histories, collaborative memoirs, and interview-based narratives often construct an autobiographical "I"/protagonist that is multiple rather than singular and that emerges across the voices of individuals whose experiences collectively narrate a shared history. Likewise, some collective life narratives depart from conventional autobiography structures, appearing as fragmented accounts, interwoven testimonies, or dispersed chapters within larger texts. This panel invites papers that examine forms of self-representation that foreground the collective. Particularly of interest are papers that interrogate the presumed unity of author, narrator, and protagonist; reconsider the autobiographical pact in light of collaborative and collective forms of narration; and explore how communities write themselves into history through shared acts of remembrance, testimony, and self-representation. Topics to consider include, but are not limited to: Collaborative and edited autobiographies Oral histories and interview-based life narratives Community and collective testimony Indigenous, Black, and diasporic collective life writing Gender, Sexuality and self testaments Archives, memory, and community self-representation Theoretical reconsiderations of the autobiographical pact and authorial voice Experimental and hybrid forms of autobiography Collective authorship, voice, and narrative authority Life writing, activism, and community formation. By centering collective modes of self-account, this panel seeks to expand the boundaries of autobiography studies and establish interventions that consider how communities, rather than individuals alone, become autobiographical subjects.

items.fullDescription: Philip Kan Gotanda's play Yankee Dawg You Die centers a conflict between two Asian American actors. The aging Vincent Chang built his career playing bumbling Japanese soldiers and one-dimensional Asian stereotypes in classic Hollywood. He's confronted by the up-and-coming Bradley Yamashita, who insists that Asian (American) characters should be played with dignity and accuracy. The heart of the play is an ambivalence about Asian (American) representation and whether viewers should sympathize with Chang for taking the only acting jobs available or with Yamashita in his condemnation of the racist roles-or with both. This panel seeks to explore comparable ambivalences in Asian/diasporic representation. We specifically seek papers about contemporary attempts to re-negotiate or reclaim depictions of Asian people, culture, literature, myth, history, etc. The papers may examine any form of media and utilize any theoretical lens, as long as the media in question seek to address anti-Asian racism or stereotypes, or to explore ambiguities in Asian/diasporic experience. "Asian" for the purposes of this panel, includes anyone (descended) from the Asian continent. By challenging imagery and cultural conceptions that stereotype or limit Asian people, this panel intends to engage the conference theme by exploring empowerments of Asian communities, whether in Asia or abroad. Please submit a 200-300 word abstract at https://cfplist.com/nemla/Home/S/22330 by 30 Sept.

items.fullDescription: Language is key to communication, whether visual, auditory, linguistic, spatial, or gestural. In literature, modalities might include devices such as phraseology, tone, sensory elements, symbolism, satire, irony, characterizations, or figurative language. Modalities in literature can be narrative, descriptive, persuasive, expository, causal, effectual, definitive, comparative, and argumentative. Through close and careful examination of "voice," "word," or "non-verbal expressions" of speaker or writer, someone might discover surprising probabilities, poignant certainties, unexpected opportunities, awakened obligations, absurd necessities, or unusual permissions that shift from "a state of passivity to being active agents of change." This roundtable session will explore, define, determine, and discuss how key modalities in various literatures have the ability to excite, intrigue, and empower communities--large and small, academic and personal, collective and individual-to values of positivity and collectiveness.

items.fullDescription: This panel seeks papers on artistic movements, individuals or events that demonstrate how living as an outsider or in exile can facilitate cross-cultural learning and creating. The call is based on ideas of reterritorializing lost, outside, and minoritarian cultures such as the Négritude movement-which was recently celebrated at the center of the Paris-Noir exhibit at the Pompidou- and what Gertrude Stein calls the Lost Generation to find empowerment in exile. We seek to share techniques of research and teaching about the power of the powerless by studying overlooked artistic communities, individuals and cultures. For example we welcome submissions about how artists of the Paris Noir and/or Lost Generation relate to issues of culture, creating and community in our time. Many are unaware that James Baldwin, Miles Davis, and Jean-Michel Basquiat worked alongside Ernest Hemingway, Gertrude Stein and Henry Miller. These expatriates and African American artists electively inhabited the creative "Undercommons" of Paris Noir to remain generative despite fascism, racism, and war. These artists who were foreigners and outsiders are now classic and read inside the classroom by a majority in culture. Submit papers that analyze minorities, foreigners, strangers, exiles and outsiders in literature, film, or culture as a model for reflecting upon ways of reterritorializing the classroom and community. Below are some questions to orient your submission, although you are not limited to working on these: How does the marginalized turn the margin to a source of empowerment? What type of artist or work demonstrates change on the margins? What does it mean to inhabit to the role of foreigner, outsider, or minority? How does the existence of the stranger, immigrant, minority, or neighbor facilitate cross-cultural dialogue and generativity? How do we inhabit and engage vulnerability, process, child-like discovery, repetition and outside as a way of innovating that resists an authoritarian or artificial rule? Hybrid: The session will be held in-person but a few remote presentations may be included. All abstract submissions must be submitted through the NeMLA portal. https://cfplist.com/nemla/ Home/S/22549 Chair: Dr. Jessica Datema, jdatema@bergen.edu, https://bergen.edu/academics/academic-divisions-departments/english-department/our-composition-literature-faculty/jessica-datema/ https://www.nemla.org/convention.html

items.location: Newport, RI Convention Site: Salve Regina University · NeMLA's 58th Annual Convention

items.relevance: Automatically surfaced as a possible RCID fit across Film / Media.
items.fullDescription: Abstract The twenty-first century is continuously defined by geopolitical shifts, armed conflict, environmental precarity, and evolving border regimes, within which migration operates as a critical locus of global transformation. This physical transgression of geopolitical boundaries inevitably instigates a profound restructuring of individual and collective subjectivities. Literature functions as a dynamic repository for these complex trajectories, articulating the psychological, political, and cultural exigencies of displacement and resettlement. Crucially, migration is a highly gendered phenomenon; an individual's positionality is negotiated and reconfigured at every stage of the diasporic process-from the structural catalysts for departure to the physical perils of transit and the socio-political parameters of host nations. Global literary traditions rigorously interrogate these intersections, offering critical frameworks to analyze the nuances of home, alienation, and hybridity. This session aims to examine how global narratives represent the friction between shifting geopolitical borders and the ontological reconstruction of the gendered self. Consequently, it intends to investigate how authors across diverse historical epochs, geographic regions, and genres textualize the quest for belonging. By transcending static regional paradigms, this session highlights the multi-faceted modalities through which literature maps human mobility. Description This session holds immense academic significance by applying an intersectional framework to global displacement. It bridges the analytical gap between individual trauma and macro-level geopolitical structures through the concept of community. Ultimately, it redefines world literature by treating textuality as a dynamic repository for marginalized subjectivities.

Proposals are welcome from a range of disciplinary and professional perspectives that explore food and cultural identity in literature, cinema, and other forms of media; oral history projects; mapping initiatives; community archives and digital collections; public-facing scholarship; and pedagogical approaches to Food Studies. The panel also invites discussion of emerging technologies and methodologies that offer new ways of researching, representing, and engaging with foodways and cultural heritage, while expanding opportunities for the communication and exchange of cultural knowledge. Whether through literary and cinematic representation, historical research, oral histories, or other forms of cultural production and inquiry, food-centred scholarship offers valuable insights into the ways individuals and communities understand and communicate culture across generations and geographies. In keeping with the conference theme, Empowering Communities, this session considers how foodways can serve as sites of cultural expression, knowledge-sharing, and collective memory, while encouraging reflection on questions of belonging, exchange, continuity, and cultural change.

items.relevance: Automatically surfaced as a possible RCID fit across Film / Media.
items.fullDescription: Childhood is often imagined as a period of innocence, protection, and dependence. However, across many historical and contemporary contexts, children experience various forms of violence, including interpersonal abuse, structural inequality, displacement, war, and environmental harm. Recent scholarship in childhood studies has challenged the tendency to view children solely as vulnerable victims, emphasizing their capacity for agency, resilience, and political engagement. Understanding how cultural narratives represent these experiences is essential for examining broader questions of justice, rights, and social belonging. Taking this into perspective, the panel seeks to explore how literature, film, visual culture, and other narrative forms depict children's encounters with interpersonal, structural, and slow violence across diverse cultural contexts, while examining how these narratives foreground children's agency, political subjectivity, and everyday practices of resistance. We invite papers that examine representations of childhood, violence, political becoming, and resistance in literature, film, media, visual culture, and related cultural forms. We seek contributions that explore how children navigate, witness, contest, and survive diverse forms of violence while emerging as ethical, political, and social actors. Topics may include, but are not limited to: child displacement and migration; political victimization; children's resistance and agency; slow and structural violence; children's literature and picturebooks; childhood in film and visual culture; biopolitics and necropolitics; and Global South perspectives on childhood and justice.

items.fullDescription: From its founding ideals framing rights as tied to individuals to many of the ‘myths that made America,’ such as the self-made man, ideas of individualism are deeply entrenched in the culture of the United States. Yet at the same time, the US has also always understood itself as a particular community: as a city upon a hill, a melting pot, a more perfect union. As such, the United States has been established on a seeming paradox, forming ‘out of many, one.’ More recently, this previous consensus has become increasingly brittle, with fragmentation, polarization, hyper-individualism, and loneliness on the rise—as some would argue: again.

items.fullDescription: Postcolonial Interventions (ISSN 2455-6564) Call for Papers Vol. XII, Issue 1 (January 2027) Postcolonial Interventions invites scholarly articles for the January 2027 issue on the theme of Popular Culture and the Rise of the Right. Across the world, right-wing governments are gaining power and momentum and various aspects of popular culture have played a key role in endorsing, proliferating and consolidating the basic tenets of these regimes which include such common features as xenophobia, persecution of minorities, religious bigotry, ecological catastrophes, jingoism, discrimination against LGBTQI+ communities, collapse of social security coupled with increased precarity, nexus between state and oligarchs, patriarchal chauvinism and much else. At the same time, the same domain of popular culture continues to generate subversive voices as well which keep on opposing the cardinal ideas of the dominant order. We are hoping to receive articles that take into account these currents and cross-currents to explore the layered and complex relationship between right-wing politics and popular culture across postcolonial societies through an examination of diverse cultural artefacts. Submission Guidelines: Articles must be original and unpublished. Submission will imply that it is not being considered for publication elsewhere. Written in Times New Roman 12, double spaced with 1″ margin on all sides, in doc/docx format. Between 4000-7000 words, inclusive of all citations. With in-text citations and a Works Cited list complying with Chicago Manual of Style (author-date) specifications. A separate cover page should include the author's name, designation, an abstract of 250 words with a maximum of 5 keywords and a short bio-note of 50 words. The main article should not in any way contain the author's name. Otherwise the article will not be considered. Reviews also need to follow the aforementioned guidelines. However, word limit for reviews is 1500 words. Only reviews of books published within the last two years is acceptable. The contributors are responsible for obtaining permission to reproduce any material, including photographs and illustrations for which they do not hold copyright. Please submit a plagiarism check report from Turnitin/Drillbit or any other reliable service provider along with your paper. Similarity percentage should be 10 or lower. Please send all your contributions to postcolonialinterventions@gmail.com within 15 October, 2026.

items.fullDescription: Call for Chapters Proposals Submission Deadline: July 17, 2026 Full Chapters Due: October 30, 2026 Submission Date: October 30, 2026 Introduction This edited collection features scholarly voices with attention to horror in its transmedia representations, including film, literature, comics, art, branding, and more. Chapters give attention to the ways that fans take up the realities and ideas presented in horror, the affordances of the genre, and participation in this network of storytelling. The reactions of readers/viewers/fans, the decisions of creatives, the presentation of ideas within particular forms of media, and more form elements of the researched conversation in this volume. Objective This collection seeks to advance conversation about the theoretical functions of horror across media including notions of adaptation and literary/literacy-based choices in the work. This includes, but is not limited to, design structures, cultural influences, the role of lore, (re)considerations of the monstrous, embodiment in the genre, sub-genre considerations, and more. Target Audience This book is intended for academics in media, literacy, literature, communication, and interdisciplinary fields. Students enrolled in courses in these fields can benefit from this knowledge as they pursue courses of study and further academic work in these disciplines. Recommended Topics Emotional and Philosophical Reactions to Horror Media Themes and Interactions across Media Representations in the Genre Adaptation Processes/Decisions/Designs in Horror across Media Studies of Narrative and Response/Interpretation Recent Trends/Movements in Horror Theoretical Approaches to Horror Composition/Representation Analysis of Race, Gender, Class in Horror Considerations and Reconsiderations of the Monstrous Author/Creator Decisions and Purposes in the Genre/across Media Horror Character and Author/Director Studies Critical and Analytical Work on Embodiment in Horror Critical and Analytical Examinations of Cultural Stories in the Genre The Role of Lore/Mythology in Horror Studies of Writing/Creating Processes in the Genre Fandom Communities, Fan Fiction, Cosplay, and Other Responses Submission Procedure Researchers and practitioners are invited to submit on or before July 17, 2026, a chapter proposal of 1,000 to 2,000 words clearly explaining the mission and concerns of his or her proposed chapter. Authors will be notified by July 31, 2026 about the status of their proposals and sent chapter guidelines.Full chapters of a minimum of 10,000 words (word count includes references and related readings) are expected to be submitted by October 30, 2026, and all interested authors must consult the guidelines for manuscript submissions at https://www.igi-global.com/publish/contributor-resources/before-you-write/ prior to submission. All submitted chapters will be reviewed on a double-anonymized review basis. Contributors may also be requested to serve as reviewers for this project. Note: There are no submission or acceptance fees for manuscripts submitted to this book publication, Theory and Practice in Horror Transmedia Fandom and Creation. All manuscripts are accepted based on a double-anonymized peer review editorial process. All proposals should be submitted through the eEditorial Discovery® online submission manager. Publisher This book is scheduled to be published by IGI Global Scientific Publishing, an international academic publisher of the "Information Science Reference", "Medical Information Science Reference", "Business Science Reference", and "Engineering Science Reference" imprints. IGI Global Scientific Publishing specializes in publishing reference books, scholarly journals, and electronic databases featuring academic research on a variety of innovative topic areas including, but not limited to, education, social science, medicine and healthcare, business and management, information science and technology, engineering, public administration, library and information science, media and communication studies, and environmental science. For additional information regarding the publisher, please visit https://www.igi-global.com. This publication is anticipated to be released in 2027. Indexing Information for Prospective Authors IGI Global Scientific Publishing meets the criteria for inclusion in major indexing services such as Scopus; however, it is important to note that all indexing decisions are made independently by these services. IGI Global Scientific Publishing books are selectively indexed by the indexing organization after publication. Indexing cannot be guaranteed for any book prior to publication, and the indexing organization has complete control over the final selection and timeline. Important Dates July 17, 2026: Proposal Submission Deadline July 31, 2026: Notification of Acceptance October 30, 2026: Full Chapter Submission January 1, 2027: Review Results Returned February 12, 2027: Final Acceptance Notification February 26, 2027: Final Chapter Submission Inquiries Jason DeHart University of Tennessee Knoxville dehartjason33@gmail.com Classifications Education; Library and Information Science; Media and Communications; Social Sciences and Humanities

items.fullDescription: In India, the displacement of Adivasis, tribals, poor, farmers, forest-dwelling and land-based people, and other marginalized communities for development and infrastructure projects like dams, railways, power projects, and bridge construction, or for capitalist accumulation like mining, resource extraction, tourism, and industry, is a common occurrence. The trajectory of such displacement has a long history, starting in the colonial period and being aggravated in the postcolonial period under the Nehruvian model of development. Although such projects are implemented for the sake of the ‘greater common good’ for all, only a few elites enjoy the benefit of these projects, depriving millions of displaced, marginalized people (Roy 1999).

items.fullDescription: Special thematic dossier 8.2 | Aesthetics, Performance, Discourse and Spectacle in the Age of Trumpism Editor: Anna Marta Marini (JFKI-Freie Universität Berlin) The special dossier is meant to delve into the evolution of (discursive, cultural, visual, and digital) aesthetics and performance that characterize Trumpism specifically, and/or cultural artifacts produced and "consumed" in the so-called Trump era. As we witness an increasing fascistization of culture-intended as a constellation of phenomena (see Strick 2018; 2021; forthcoming 2026) that shape current cultural expressions in the context of the US political landscape-many have theorized for example in relation to the "Trump effect" (see Messham-Muir and Uroš Cvoro 2022) on culture, the evolution of the "dreampolitik of Trumpism" (Kennedy 2025), the articulations of the "Trump carnival" (see Gaufman and Ganesh 2024). The approach sought by the dossier encompasses a wide prolific spectrum of diverse and opposing discourses and cultural expressions. Some might align with the current administration's stances, articulating the White supremacist aspiration to collective participation "in the fantasy of [a] regression to the primal scene of their white settler ancestors' acts of dispossession and reappropriation" (Pease 2020, 26). Some might instead tap into memetic aesthetics (see Alican 2024) to counter such discourses, propelling activist action and resistance into the public digital and cultural sphere. Likewise, the shaping of mainstream popular culture, as well as independent creation, has inevitably been influenced by the evolution of the political climate since the Obama administrations. Possible topics may be (but are not limited to): - film, TV series, games, graphic narratives, literature, theater and art performance that resonate with (whether aligning with or in response to) the evolution of cultural Trumpism - memeification of politics - forms of resistance against fascistization - activism as performance, meme material, and viral circulation - the evolution of kitsch aesthetics in 21st-century US culture - gender roles and characterization, as well as queer and trans representation, in the Trump era - the (re)shaping of transgression, boundaries, and the "unthinkable" - the performance of "sincerity," authenticity and truthfulness - celebrity and performed political stances - the use of generative AI and the resulting aesthetic trends - the performative appeal and expression of conspiracy, paranoia, and truthiness - foreign cultural expressions that draw on current US trends - iterations of the grotesque and escalations of aesthetic excess - reinforcement, reclamation, reshaping of identities in response to Trumpism - exclusionary / exclusionary discourses and representations Deadline for submission of full papers: November 1, 2026 | To be published in vol 8 no 2 (May 2027) Submission guidelines REDEN accepts proposals of full articles (6000-7000 words approximately including references) about any aspect related to the call. For any inquiry or doubts about the call's topics, refer to revista.reden@uah.es. Please, upload your article to https://erevistas.publicaciones.uah.es/ojs/index.php/reden/index as a single file (.doc, .docx, .odt). Keep in mind that you will have to upload an abstract and keywords as well during the submission process. You can find the guidelines to prepare your paper, as indicated using the latest Chicago manual of style and author-date citation system, here: https://erevistas.publicaciones.uah.es/ojs/index.php/reden/about/submissions

items.fullDescription: Call for Papers - Volume 37, Issue 1 & 2 (2027) Dear Colleagues, Litera: Journal of Language, Literature and Culture Studies - Dil, Edebiyat ve Kültür Ara?t?rmalar? Dergisi, the official publication of the Department of Western Languages and Literatures at Istanbul University, invites submissions for its 2026 issues (Volume 36, Issues 1 & 2). An open-access, peer-reviewed, and multilingual journal published twice a year (June & December), Litera is indexed in ELSEVIER SCOPUS, Web of Science ESCI, MLA International Bibliography, DOAJ (Directory of Open Access Journals), ERIH PLUS, EBSCO Communication Source, EBSCO Central & Eastern European Academic Source, Gale Cengage and Ulrichsweb. While our core focus remains on literary studies, linguistics, and cultural studies, we welcome interdisciplinary approaches that combine literary and linguistic analysis with insights from cultural theory and media studies-provided they maintain a clear relevance to our aim and scope We welcome original research articles that engage with current debates and critical issues in the study of Western (American, English, French, Belgian, German, Swiss, Austrian, Italian, Spanish, Latin American and Modern Greek) languages, literatures, and cultures. In particular, we encourage contributions that reflect cutting-edge scholarship in areas such as, but not limited to: · Contemporary literary theory · Disability Studies · Human-Animal Studies · Posthumanism and critical posthumanities · Ecofeminism and ecocriticism · Ecosemiotics and Ecolinguistics · Discourse Studies · Intersectional and decolonial approaches · Digital humanities and media theory · Film Studies · Medieval Studies · Posthuman Communication Studies · Gender studies · Narrative and identity in contemporary fiction · Transnationalism and migration literature · Crete studies Submission Windows: · Issue 1 (June 2027): November 1-30, 2026 · Issue 2 (December 2027): May 1-31, 2027 Submission Guidelines: Manuscripts must be original, unpublished, and not under review elsewhere. Re-use of thesis content, dissertations, or previously published conference material is not permitted. All submissions are subject to a double-blind peer reviewprocess. Languages of Publication: English, German, French, Italian, and Spanish No processing or publication fees. Eligibility: Submissions are accepted from authors who hold a PhD. For more information, visit our journal homepage. We look forward to receiving your innovative and thought-provoking research. Sincerely, Editorial Team Litera: Journal of Language, Literature and Culture Studies

items.fullDescription: Journal of Studies in Language, Culture and Society (JSLCS) ISSN / E-ISSN: 2716-9189 / 2676-1750 Publisher: University of Bejaia Editor-in-chief: Prof. Nadia Idri, Author Guidelines for JSLCS Standards Our Goal: To be a leading international, multidisciplinary forum for high-quality research. To achieve this, we require your active partnership in building a rigorous, visible, and influential scholarly community. 1. Core Philosophy: Quality, Originality, and International Contribution To be considered, your manuscript must make a clear, original, and significant contribution to its field on an international level. We prioritize research that engages with global scholarly conversations, not just regional or national discussions. Ask yourself: Why would a scholar on another continent find this paper important? 2. Abstract: Your International Showcase The abstract is your most critical tool for attracting an international readership. Length & Detail: Provide a structured abstract of 300-350 words. Use clear headings: Objective, Methodology, Results, Conclusion, Significance/Contribution. Content: Explicitly state the paper's novelty and its significance to the international research community. Avoid generic statements. Specify the gap you fill and how your findings advance the field globally. Language: Must be in excellent, native-level English. Consider professional editing if needed. This is non-negotiable for discoverability. 3. Manuscript Scope & "The Identity Challenge" JSLCS is multidisciplinary, but your paper must have a sharp, focused identity. Connect to Core Themes: While we cover language, culture, and society, your manuscript must clearly fit within one or two defined sub-fields. Justify Broad Relevance: If your topic is regionally specific (e.g., a case study in Algeria), the Introduction and Conclusion must explicitly articulate its theoretical, methodological, or comparative implications for international research. Avoid being "a paper of everything": We will desk-reject manuscripts that are overly broad, lack a clear theoretical framework, or do not connect to specific scholarly debates. 4. References & Engagement with the Global Scholarly Community Your reference list is a map of your scholarly engagement and is crucial for our global profile. Volume & Recency: A minimum of 25-30 high-quality references is expected. A significant majority should be from the last 5 years, demonstrating engagement with current discourse. Source Quality: Prioritize references from Scopus/Web of Science-indexed journals and reputable international academic presses (Routledge, Springer, Palgrave, John Benjamins, etc.). International Dialogue: Your literature review must engage with key international scholars and seminal works in your field, not just literature from a single region. Style: Strict adherence to APA 7th Edition. 5. Authorship & Affiliation International Collaboration: Research conducted by international teams or comparing multiple contexts is highly encouraged. Institutional Email & ORCID: All corresponding authors must provide an institutional email address and a valid ORCID ID. This is mandatory for submission. Author Profiles: Authors are encouraged to maintain active, public scholarly profiles (e.g., Google Scholar, Scopus Author ID, institutional webpage). 6. Language and Accessibility for an International Audience The full manuscript must be submitted in English. To enhance accessibility, we recommend that authors of highly regionally-focused studies consider including an extended summary (500-600 words) in the article's appendix, detailing the context and international relevance for non-specialists. 7. Ethical Rigor & Transparency AI Use Disclosure: Any use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) tools in the writing, editing, or analysis process must be transparently declared in the methodology or acknowledgments section. Describe the tool used and its purpose. AI cannot be listed as an author. Plagiarism Check: All submissions will be screened. Similarity must be below 20% (excluding references). Ethical Compliance: Studies involving humans must include proof of ethics approval. Submission Checklist: Before submitting via ASJP, ensure your manuscript: Makes a clear international contribution. Has a detailed, structured abstract highlighting novelty and significance. Fits a defined niche within our broad scope. Contains 25-30+ references (can be more), mostly recent and from high-indexed sources. Engages with international literature. Is written in excellent, polished English. Uses the correct template and APA 7th style. Includes author ORCID iD and institutional email. Has a declaration on AI use (if applicable). Submission via ASJP https://asjp.cerist.dz/en/PresentationRevue/681 More information: https://univ-bejaia.dz/jslcs

items.fullDescription: Cuteness is a twenty-first-century sensation—from the global expansion of Sanrio fandom to the rise of social capital around Labubu and Jellycat, from K-Pop stars (real or animated) to cute animals like the quokka and Fennec fox. This issue explores a “cuteness turn” found in many public realms around the world. The adoration of attractive or cheerful objects associated with childishness and youthfulness is part of the human experience. However, this issue does not examine cuteness as a transcultural aesthetic or psychological condition, but we focus on its larger political implications. Throughout history and around the world, this “universal” sentiment has often been manipulated for commercial and ideological purposes.

items.fullDescription: In the wake of ecocriticism and environmental humanities, the blue humanities have emerged as a field of study that emphasizes the centrality of aquatic environments in understanding interactions between humans and nonhumans. This interdisciplinary field, which initially grew out of Anglophone literary criticism, proposes to shift our terra-centric perspective by adopting the seas and oceans as a new vantage point to rethink our understanding of both the planet and literature (Klein, 2002; Blum, 2008; Bailyn, 2005). Originally developed in the United States within the field of Oceanic studies (Blum 2010; Cohen 2010; Mentz 2009), the “Blue studies” have since expanded and become increasingly decentered and diversified.

items.fullDescription: How is everyone doing? At a routine meeting of the Editorial Board of this journal in March 2026, this innocuous question erupted into disquiet, tension, unease. Speaking from vastly different parts of the world, there was an unmistakable sense of a planet slipping beyond available comprehension and unplugging us from work and life routines. As wars in the Middle East expand and fires spread from apartment blocks reduced to rubble to bombed oil refineries and shipping corridors, profound geopolitical, personal, even psychic rifts leave us grasping for the language and feel of the moment, the falling apart of things, the zeitgeist of an endangered now.

items.fullDescription: The advancement of artificial intelligence has transformed humanities research and education, deepening computation’s influence on scholarly practice and everyday life. From the early era of “humanities computing” in the 1970s to the rise of “computational humanities” over the past decade, this trajectory highlights the enduring—and expanding—role of computation in shaping inquiry across the humanities. These intersections are especially visible in interdisciplinary work. As T. S. Eliot observes, “Immature poets imitate; mature poets steal; bad poets deface what they take, and good poets make it into something better, or at least something different.” The same spirit can illuminate how methods and tools migrate across fields.

items.fullDescription: Our conference is a global online gathering of educators, instructional designers, learning innovators, researchers, and industry leaders dedicated to shaping the future of learning. LXD has emerged as a powerful bridge between learning design and educational technology, combining pedagogical expertise with technological innovation to create engaging, learnercentred experiences. This conference provides a unique platform for sharing cutting-edge practices, innovative approaches, and emerging trends in LXD across diverse educational and professional contexts. The conference programme includes keynote presentations, interactive workshops, panel discussions, session meetings and networking opportunities. We invite contributions from the following fields: 1) Learning Experience Design Foundations; 2) Learning Sciences and Pedagogy; 3) EdTech and Digital Innovation; 4) AI and the Future of Learning; 5) User Experience and Interface Design for Learning; 6) Workplace Learning and Professional Development; 7) Inclusion, Equity, and Accessibility. PARTICIPATION Conference language is English. To participate in the conference, please fill in a registration form on our website https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/ by March 05, 2027. The confirmation of conference participation will be sent within two weeks from the submission date but not later than March 12, 2027. Conference fee: 100 CHF. For participation in the conference both speakers and listeners will be awarded with ECTS credits (1 ECTS credit for speakers and 0,5 ECTS credits for listeners), which will be stated in the certificate of attendance. Certificates of attendance will be issued to all participants, including speakers and attendees, contingent upon their physical presence at the conference. REQUIREMENTS FOR SPEAKERS Agenda: plenary speeches - 30 minutes, section speeches - 15 minutes; discussion time - 10 minutes. PUBLICATION OF ABSTRACTS All conference participants may submit abstracts for publication. Abstracts will be published in the e-Book of Abstracts containing ISBN and DOI numbers. The language of publication is English. The deadline for abstracts submission is March 05, 2027. Abstracts must be downloaded in the registration form on our website: https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/. The abstract must contain 250-300 words, excluding references. The abstract must be preceded by the name(s) of the author(s), including their title(s), affiliation, and country (all in the abovementioned order). The title of the abstract must be in bold, all capitals, centered. References, if applicable (presented in the end of the abstract), and in-text citations must conform to the APA Style. All submitted abstracts will be subjected to the double-blind peer review process. The editorial board reserves the right to decline papers that contain plagiarized content or do not conform to the norms and subject matter of the conference. ENQUIRIES For any questions concerning the conference, please contact Dr. Nataliia Gach at info@ldinitiatives.ch or by phone: +41 78 340 32 13. For more information visit our website https://ld-initiatives.ch/en/conferences/.

The primary aim of the journal is to help accumulate knowledge about how foreign languages, cultures, and literatures can change students' lives. The journal is only electronic (no print version). It is biannually published in April and October.

items.fullDescription: The aim of this book series is to examine the history of institutions around the world through the lens of gender. Of interest are institutions established with the specific purpose of regulating gender and sexuality (e.g. the Magdalen asylums, Magdalen hospitals, penitentiaries, refuges, mother and baby institutions) as well as those with more general purposes where gender has had an important role in their operation and function (e.g. prisons, workhouses, lunatic asylums), including both religious and private organisations. The series supports the increasing interest in these institutions internationally, both in academia and in the treatment of ‘historical’ abuse.
